questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled and the contract pays the sum. One die is revealed as 1; the other stays hidden. Updated fair value?

explanations.A: Correct. The revealed die is 1 and the hidden fair die averages 3.5, so fair = 1 + 3.5 = 4.5.
explanations.B: 1 is only the revealed die, not the expected sum of both dice.
explanations.C: 7 is the unconditional two-dice mean; it ignores the reveal of 1.
explanations.D: This rounds the hidden die down to 3, but a fair die averages 3.5.

explanations.D: 6.5 is a guess at the pre-reveal mean; no uncertainty remains once the card is shown.

questions.question: A card is drawn and you are told it is red. It pays $10 if it is a heart, $0 otherwise. Fair value?

explanations.A: 2.5 is the unconditional EV before the color clue, when P(heart) = 1/4.
explanations.B: 10 assumes the card is certainly a heart.
explanations.C: Correct. Among the 26 red cards, the 13 hearts make P(heart | red) = 1/2, so EV = 10 × 1/2 = 5.
explanations.D: 0 ignores the 50% heart chance among red cards.

questions.question: A card is drawn and you are told it is black. It pays $20 if it is a club, $0 otherwise. Fair value?

explanations.A: 5 is the unconditional EV before the color clue, when P(club) = 1/4.
explanations.B: 20 assumes the card is certainly a club.
explanations.C: 0 ignores the 1/2 club chance among black cards.
explanations.D: Correct. Half of the 26 black cards are the 13 clubs, so EV = 20 × 1/2 = 10.

questions.question: A card is drawn and you are told it is a face card (J, Q, or K). It pays $30 if it is a Queen. Fair value?

explanations.A: Correct. Among the three face ranks, P(Queen | face) = 1/3, so EV = 30 × 1/3 = 10.
explanations.B: 30 assumes the card is certainly a Queen.
explanations.C: 7.5 wrongly uses 1/4 instead of 1/3.
explanations.D: 0 ignores the 1/3 Queen chance among face cards.

questions.question: A card is drawn and you are told its rank is even (2,4,6,8,10). It pays $10 if the rank is exactly 10. Fair value?

explanations.A: 10 assumes the card is certainly a 10.
explanations.B: Correct. Five even ranks are equally likely, so P(10) = 1/5 and EV = 10 × 1/5 = 2.
explanations.C: 5 uses 1/2 instead of 1/5.
explanations.D: 0 ignores the 1/5 chance of a 10.

questions.question: A card is drawn and you are told its rank is at most 4 (A,2,3,4). It pays $8 if it is an Ace. Fair value?

explanations.A: 8 assumes the card is certainly an Ace.
explanations.B: 4 uses 1/2 instead of 1/4.
explanations.C: Correct. Four ranks are equally likely, so P(Ace) = 1/4 and EV = 8 × 1/4 = 2.
explanations.D: 0 ignores the 1/4 Ace chance.

questions.question: A card is drawn from a full deck and you are told it is red. What is P(heart | red)?

explanations.A: 1/4 is the unconditional P(heart) before the color clue.
explanations.B: 1/13 is the probability of a specific rank, not a suit given color.
explanations.C: 1/26 is the chance of one specific red card.
explanations.D: Correct. There are 13 hearts among the 26 red cards, so 13/26 = 1/2.

questions.question: A coin is either fair (P(heads) = 1/2) or double-headed (P(heads) = 1), each equally likely. You flip it once and see heads. What is P(double-headed | heads)?

explanations.B: 1/2 is the prior; it ignores that heads is evidence for the double-headed coin.
explanations.C: 1/3 inverts the Bayes ratio.
explanations.D: Heads can also come from the fair coin, so it is not certain.

explanations.A: 13 assumes the card is certainly the Ace of spades.
explanations.B: 0.25 ≈ 13/52 is the unconditional EV, ignoring the spade clue.
explanations.C: Correct. Given a spade, there are 13 equally likely ranks, so P(Ace) = 1/13 and EV = 13 × 1/13 = 1.
explanations.D: 0 ignores the 1/13 chance of the Ace given a spade.

questions.question: Two fair dice are rolled. You are told the sum is exactly 4. It pays $6 if the dice were (2,2). Fair value?

explanations.A: 3 wrongly treats only two outcomes (uses 1/2).
explanations.B: 6 assumes (2,2) is certain.
explanations.C: 0 ignores the 1/3 chance of (2,2) given sum 4.
explanations.D: Correct. Sum 4 happens via (1,3),(2,2),(3,1) — three equally likely ordered pairs — so P((2,2)) = 1/3 and EV = 6 × 1/3 = 2.

questions.question: Two-dice sum game (unconditional fair 7). You learn only that the sum is at least 10. How should fair value move?
options: Rise above 7, since low sums are ruled out.
Stay at 7, since no exact sum is known.
Become exactly 10.
Fall below 7.

explanations.A: Correct. Conditioning on sum ≥ 10 removes all low outcomes, pulling the conditional mean well above 7.
explanations.B: Partial info still reshapes the distribution; ruling out low sums raises the mean.
explanations.C: 'At least 10' is a lower bound, not the exact sum.
explanations.D: Removing low outcomes can only raise, never lower, the mean.

questions.question: Two-dice sum game (unconditional fair 7). You learn only that the sum is at most 4. How should fair value move?
options: Stay at 7.
Fall below 7, since only low sums remain.
Become exactly 4.
Rise above 7.

explanations.A: Capping the sum changes the distribution, so the mean must move.
explanations.B: Correct. Truncating away high sums pulls the conditional mean below 7.
explanations.C: 'At most 4' is an upper bound, not the exact value.
explanations.D: Removing high outcomes cannot raise the mean.

questions.question: A contract pays the number of heads in two fair coins. You learn at least one coin is heads. How does fair value move versus the unconditional 1.0?
options: Stay at 1.0.
Become exactly 2.
Rise above 1.0, since the all-tails case is removed.
Fall below 1.0.

explanations.A: Removing the zero-heads case shifts the distribution upward.
explanations.B: At least one head does not force both to be heads.
explanations.C: Correct. Conditioning on ≥1 head deletes the (0,0) outcome, raising expected heads above 1.
explanations.D: Eliminating the lowest outcome cannot lower the mean.

questions.question: Two-dice sum game. You learn only that the sum is even. How should the fair value of 7 change?
options: Rise above 7.
Fall below 7.
Become exactly 6.
Stay at 7, since the even sums are symmetric around 7.

explanations.A: The even sums are symmetric, so there is no upward pull.
explanations.B: The even sums are symmetric, so there is no downward pull.
explanations.C: 6 ignores the symmetry of the even sums around 7.
explanations.D: Correct. Even sums {2,4,...,12} are symmetric about 7, so the conditional mean is still 7.

questions.question: In general, learning a strict lower bound on an uncertain payoff (and nothing else) does what to conditional fair value?
options: Raises it, since low outcomes are removed.
Always lowers it.
Never changes it.
Sets it to zero.

explanations.A: Correct. Truncating away the lowest outcomes can only increase the conditional mean.
explanations.B: A lower bound removes small outcomes, so it raises the mean.
explanations.C: Removing possible outcomes changes the distribution and its mean.
explanations.D: There is no reason fair value collapses to zero.

questions.question: In general, learning a strict upper bound on an uncertain payoff (and nothing else) does what to conditional fair value?
options: Always raises it.
Lowers it, since high outcomes are removed.
Never changes it.
Sets it to the bound.

explanations.A: An upper bound removes large outcomes, so it lowers the mean.
explanations.B: Correct. Truncating away the largest outcomes can only decrease the conditional mean.
explanations.C: Removing possible outcomes changes the distribution and its mean.
explanations.D: An upper bound is a cap, not the exact realized value.

questions.question: Two-dice sum game. You learn the sum is at least 8. Which fair value is plausible?
options: Exactly 8.
Still 7.
Around 9.7 — clearly above 7.
Below 7.

explanations.A: 'At least 8' is a lower bound, not the exact value.
explanations.B: Removing sums below 8 raises the conditional mean above 7.
explanations.C: Correct. Conditioning on sum ≥ 8 keeps only {8,9,10,11,12}, whose probability-weighted mean is about 9.7, well above 7.
explanations.D: Removing low sums cannot lower the mean.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is even, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.B: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.C: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.
explanations.D: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {2, 4, 6}, whose average is 4.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is odd, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {1, 3, 5}, whose average is 3.
explanations.B: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.C: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.D: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is at least 3, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.B: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {3, 4, 5, 6}, whose average is 4.5.
explanations.C: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.D: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is at most 4, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.B: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.C: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {1, 2, 3, 4}, whose average is 2.5.
explanations.D: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is at least 4, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.B: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.C: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.
explanations.D: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {4, 5, 6}, whose average is 5.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is at most 2, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {1, 2}, whose average is 1.5.
explanations.B: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.C: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.D: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is greater than 1, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.B: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {2, 3, 4, 5, 6}, whose average is 4.
explanations.C: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.D: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is less than 6, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.B: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.C: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}, whose average is 3.
explanations.D: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is at least 5, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.B: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.C: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.
explanations.D: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {5, 6}, whose average is 5.5.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is at most 3, what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {1, 2, 3}, whose average is 2.
explanations.B: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.C: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.D: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is a prime number (2, 3, or 5), what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.B: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {2, 3, 5}, whose average is 3.333.
explanations.C: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.D: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.

questions.question: A single fair die is rolled. Given that the face is a perfect square (1 or 4), what is the expected face value?

explanations.A: 3.5 is the unconditional die mean, ignoring the given condition.
explanations.B: That is the smallest allowed face, not the average of the allowed faces.
explanations.C: Correct. The remaining equally likely faces are {1, 4}, whose average is 2.5.
explanations.D: That is the largest allowed face, not the average.

questions.question: Fair was 7 on a two-dice sum. One die is revealed as 4, but you leave your quote centered at 7. What will a sharp trader do?
options: Hit your bid — your bid is too high.
Nothing — your quote is still fine.
Trade both sides equally.
Lift your offer — your ask is now well below the new fair, so they buy from you cheaply.

explanations.A: Fair rose to 7.5, so your bid is actually too low, not too high; they lift your offer instead.
explanations.B: The reveal moved fair to 7.5; a quote centered at 7 is stale and losable.
explanations.C: The mispricing is one-directional, so the edge is only on one side.
explanations.D: Correct. New fair = 4 + 3.5 = 7.5 > 7, so your stale ask near 7 is too cheap and gets lifted.

questions.question: Fair was 7 on a two-dice sum. One die is revealed as 5, but you leave your quote centered at 7. What will a sharp trader do?
options: Lift your offer — your ask is now well below the new fair, so they buy from you cheaply.
Hit your bid — your bid is too high.
Nothing — your quote is still fine.
Trade both sides equally.

explanations.A: Correct. New fair = 5 + 3.5 = 8.5 > 7, so your stale ask near 7 is too cheap and gets lifted.
explanations.B: Fair rose to 8.5, so your bid is actually too low, not too high; they lift your offer instead.
explanations.C: The reveal moved fair to 8.5; a quote centered at 7 is stale and losable.
explanations.D: The mispricing is one-directional, so the edge is only on one side.

questions.question: Fair was 7 on a two-dice sum. One die is revealed as 6, but you leave your quote centered at 7. What will a sharp trader do?
options: Hit your bid — your bid is too high.
Lift your offer — your ask is now well below the new fair, so they buy from you cheaply.
Nothing — your quote is still fine.
Trade both sides equally.

explanations.A: Fair rose to 9.5, so your bid is actually too low, not too high; they lift your offer instead.
explanations.B: Correct. New fair = 6 + 3.5 = 9.5 > 7, so your stale ask near 7 is too cheap and gets lifted.
explanations.C: The reveal moved fair to 9.5; a quote centered at 7 is stale and losable.
explanations.D: The mispricing is one-directional, so the edge is only on one side.

questions.question: Fair was 7 on a two-dice sum. One die is revealed as 1, but you leave your quote centered at 7. What will a sharp trader do?
options: Lift your offer — your ask is too cheap.
Nothing — your quote is still fine.
Hit your bid — your bid is now well above the new fair, so they sell to you richly.
Trade both sides equally.

explanations.A: Fair fell to 4.5, so your offer is rich, not cheap; they hit your bid instead.
explanations.B: The reveal moved fair to 4.5; a quote centered at 7 is stale and losable.
explanations.C: Correct. New fair = 1 + 3.5 = 4.5 < 7, so your stale bid near 7 is too high and gets hit.
explanations.D: The mispricing is one-directional, so the edge is only on one side.

questions.question: Fair was 7 on a two-dice sum. One die is revealed as 2, but you leave your quote centered at 7. What will a sharp trader do?
options: Lift your offer — your ask is too cheap.
Nothing — your quote is still fine.
Trade both sides equally.
Hit your bid — your bid is now well above the new fair, so they sell to you richly.

explanations.A: Fair fell to 5.5, so your offer is rich, not cheap; they hit your bid instead.
explanations.B: The reveal moved fair to 5.5; a quote centered at 7 is stale and losable.
explanations.C: The mispricing is one-directional, so the edge is only on one side.
explanations.D: Correct. New fair = 2 + 3.5 = 5.5 < 7, so your stale bid near 7 is too high and gets hit.

questions.question: Fair was 7 on a two-dice sum. One die is revealed as 3, but you leave your quote centered at 7. What will a sharp trader do?
options: Hit your bid — your bid is now well above the new fair, so they sell to you richly.
Lift your offer — your ask is too cheap.
Nothing — your quote is still fine.
Trade both sides equally.

explanations.A: Correct. New fair = 3 + 3.5 = 6.5 < 7, so your stale bid near 7 is too high and gets hit.
explanations.B: Fair fell to 6.5, so your offer is rich, not cheap; they hit your bid instead.
explanations.C: The reveal moved fair to 6.5; a quote centered at 7 is stale and losable.
explanations.D: The mispricing is one-directional, so the edge is only on one side.

questions.question: A public clue moves fair value from 5 to 8. You reprice in 2 seconds; a rival reprices instantly. Who gets picked off?
options: The rival — repricing fast is a mistake.
You — your stale quote sits at the old fair longer.
Neither — fair value is irrelevant.
Both equally — speed does not matter.

explanations.A: Fast repricing reduces, not increases, pick-off risk.
explanations.B: Correct. Slow repricing leaves your mispriced quote exposed, and the fast rival (or other traders) trade against it.
explanations.C: Fair value drives correct pricing; ignoring it is exactly what gets you picked off.
explanations.D: With public info, speed determines who is left holding a stale quote.

questions.question: When everyone sees the same public clue at the same time, your main edge is:
options: Secret knowledge of fair value.
Quoting wider than everyone else.
Speed of repricing and spread discipline.
Refusing to update your quote.

explanations.A: If the clue is public, no one has secret knowledge of its implication.
explanations.B: Excess width just cedes flow; the edge is speed, not blanket widening.
explanations.C: Correct. Shared information leaves no secret knowledge; the advantage is repricing first and managing spread.
explanations.D: Not updating leaves you with the stale quote that gets picked off.

questions.question: A 'stale' quote is best described as one that:
options: Simply has a wide spread.
Is always crossed with the other side.
Exactly matches current fair value.
Does not reflect the latest available information.

explanations.A: A wide quote can still be fresh; width is not the same as staleness.
explanations.B: A crossed market is a different error, not the definition of stale.
explanations.C: That describes a fresh, correct quote, the opposite of stale.
explanations.D: Correct. Staleness is about lagging information; the quote is centered on outdated fair value.

questions.question: Immediately after new information arrives, your first action should be to:
options: Recompute fair value and re-center your quote.
Only widen the spread and leave the center.
Never change your quotes.
Deliberately cross your own market.

explanations.A: Correct. Repricing the midpoint to the new conditional expectation is the priority before anything else.
explanations.B: Widening without re-centering still leaves a mis-centered, losable quote.
explanations.C: Leaving quotes unchanged is exactly how you get picked off.
explanations.D: Crossing your market is nonsensical and not a response to information.

questions.question: Two market makers see the same clue. One reprices instantly; the other lags by a second. The laggard most likely:
options: Earns more because they trade more slowly.
Offers stale prices that the fast trader can pick off.
Is unaffected because both saw the clue.
Automatically has a tighter spread.

explanations.A: Slower repricing increases adverse fills, not profit.
explanations.B: Correct. During the lag the laggard's quote reflects old fair value and is exposed to being traded against.
explanations.C: Seeing the clue is not enough; acting on it quickly is what protects you.
explanations.D: Lagging does not tighten a spread; it just leaves it mispriced.

questions.question: Why is raw speed an edge specifically when information is public?
options: Because speed creates private information.
Because fast quotes are always wider.
Because the math is shared, so whoever reprices first avoids being the stale quote.
Because slow traders always quote better prices.

explanations.A: Speed does not manufacture private information; the clue is still public.
explanations.B: Speed and width are independent; speed is about timing.
explanations.C: Correct. With public info the conditional value is the same for all, so the only differentiator is acting first.
explanations.D: Slow traders quote stale prices, which are worse, not better.

questions.question: After a clue you adjust only your spread width but leave the midpoint at the old fair. The result is:
options: A fully corrected, safe quote.
No difference from doing nothing.
Guaranteed profit on both sides.
A mis-centered quote that is still exposed to pick-off.

explanations.A: Without re-centering, the quote remains wrong on the side the news favored.
explanations.B: Widening helps a little, but the uncorrected center is the core problem.
explanations.C: A mis-centered quote tends to lose on the side the information favored.
explanations.D: Correct. If the midpoint is wrong, one side is still mispriced regardless of how wide you make the spread.

questions.question: The best summary of repricing discipline under partial information is:
options: Treat every new clue as mandatory, immediate repricing.
Reprice only when you feel like it.
Reprice once per session regardless of clues.
Never reprice to avoid mistakes.

explanations.A: Correct. Each clue changes the conditional expectation, so quotes must move at once to stay fair.
explanations.B: Discretionary, delayed repricing is exactly what creates stale quotes.
explanations.C: Information arrives continuously; one update per session leaves you stale most of the time.
explanations.D: Not repricing guarantees the mistake of holding a stale quote.

questions.question: Your market is 9.9 bid / 10.1 ask. Buyers repeatedly lift your 10.1 offer and no one hits your bid. The most likely read is:
options: Random noise — keep the same quote.
Informed buying — value is above your offer; raise your fair estimate and reprice up.
Value is below your quote — lower your fair.
Just widen the bid and keep selling at 10.1.

explanations.A: Persistent one-sided flow is information, not noise; ignoring it invites repeated pick-offs.
explanations.B: Correct. Persistent one-sided buying is a signal that fair value sits above 10.1, so you should lift your fair and quotes.
explanations.C: One-sided buying points to higher value, not lower.
explanations.D: The offer side is being hit cheaply; you must lift fair, not only adjust the bid.

questions.question: Sellers keep hitting your 9.9 bid and no one lifts your offer. The signal is:
options: Value is above your quote — raise your fair.
It is meaningless noise — do nothing.
Informed selling — value is below your bid; lower your fair and reprice down.
Just raise the offer and keep buying at 9.9.

explanations.A: One-sided selling points to lower value, not higher.
explanations.B: Sustained one-sided flow is a signal; ignoring it lets you keep buying too high.
explanations.C: Correct. Persistent selling into your bid suggests fair value is under 9.9, so you should mark your fair down.
explanations.D: Your bid is the side being hit richly; you must lower fair, not only move the offer.

questions.question: Size starts lifting offers across the market just before a scheduled reveal. This flow suggests:
options: Nothing — pre-reveal flow is always uninformed.
You should cut your offer to win the trades.
You should lower your fair value.
Some traders may have an information edge; shade your offer up before requoting.

explanations.A: Flow concentrated right before news is often the most informed flow, not the least.
explanations.B: Cutting your offer just hands cheap size to possibly-informed buyers.
explanations.C: Buying pressure points to higher, not lower, fair value.
explanations.D: Correct. Pre-reveal one-sided buying can reflect superior information, so protect yourself by lifting your offer.

questions.question: Flow is roughly balanced: about as many lifts as hits, in small size. The right inference is:
options: No clear directional signal; keep your fair centered.
Strong buy signal — raise fair sharply.
Strong sell signal — drop fair sharply.
Stop quoting entirely.

explanations.A: Correct. Balanced, small two-sided flow looks uninformed, so there is little reason to move fair.
explanations.B: Balanced flow shows no directional pressure to justify a big upward move.
explanations.C: Balanced flow shows no directional pressure to justify a big downward move.
explanations.D: Uninformed two-sided flow is exactly the business you want to make; no reason to step away.

questions.question: You get lifted once, in small size, with no follow-through. How much should you update fair value?
options: Massively — treat it as certain information.
Only slightly, if at all — one small print is weak evidence.
Lower fair value.
Not at all, ever, under any flow.

explanations.A: One small print is weak evidence and does not justify a large repricing.
explanations.B: Correct. A single small fill is noisy; a large fair-value jump would over-react to thin information.
explanations.C: A buy lifting your offer points up, not down.
explanations.D: Flow is informative; the point is to scale the update to the evidence, not ignore it.

questions.question: Your offer is lifted in progressively larger size at higher and higher prices. This pattern is:
options: Weaker evidence than a single small print.
A reason to lower your fair value.
Strong evidence of informed buying; update fair meaningfully higher.
Irrelevant as long as you keep your spread.

explanations.A: Larger, persistent, price-paying flow is stronger, not weaker, evidence.
explanations.B: Aggressive buying points to higher, not lower, value.
explanations.C: Correct. Growing size paying up across levels is a strong directional signal that fair value is rising.
explanations.D: Holding a stale fair while size pays up is how you get run over.

questions.question: A seller dumps large size into your bid at successively lower prices. Your best response is:
options: Keep your bid and buy all the size.
Raise your fair value.
Only widen the offer side.
Lower your fair value and your bid; your bid was too high.

explanations.A: Buying everything at a stale-high bid into informed selling bleeds edge.
explanations.B: Heavy selling points to lower value, not higher.
explanations.C: The bid is the side being hit; you must mark the bid (and fair) down.
explanations.D: Correct. Aggressive selling at falling prices signals lower fair value, so mark down to avoid buying too rich.

questions.question: You raise your offer in response to buying, yet buyers keep lifting the new, higher offer. This implies:
options: Fair value is still above your offer; keep repricing higher.
You have already over-priced; cut the offer.
The buying must now be uninformed.
You should stop updating and hold.

explanations.A: Correct. Continued lifting after a raise means your fair update was too small relative to the information.
explanations.B: If buyers keep paying up, the offer is still too low, not too high.
explanations.C: Persistent willingness to pay even higher is, if anything, stronger evidence of information.
explanations.D: Holding a still-too-low offer keeps gifting cheap size to informed buyers.

questions.question: Compared with a public clue everyone sees, private order flow is special because:
options: It is always less informative than public news.
It can reveal information not yet public, letting you update fair before others.
It never justifies changing your quote.
It only matters for the exchange, not the maker.

explanations.A: Order flow often contains information ahead of any public announcement.
explanations.B: Correct. Reading informed flow gives an edge precisely because that information is not yet in the public price.
explanations.C: Informed flow is one of the main reasons to update quotes.
explanations.D: Makers live or die by reading flow; it is central to repricing.

questions.question: The best general response to persistent, clearly informed buying is to:
options: Keep your quote fixed and trade more size.
Lower your offer to capture more flow.
Raise your quotes and/or widen the offer side to stop selling below true value.
Lower your fair value to match the buying.

explanations.A: Trading more at a stale fair multiplies your losses to informed flow.
explanations.B: Lowering the offer hands informed buyers even cheaper size.
explanations.C: Correct. Repricing up and protecting the offer reduces how much edge you give to informed buyers.
explanations.D: Buying pressure implies higher value, so lowering fair is backwards.

questions.question: Order flow is informative mainly because:
options: Trades are always perfectly random.
Only the exchange can see trades.
Prices never reflect trading activity.
Trades reveal what counterparties are willing to do at the price, which can carry information.

explanations.A: If flow were purely random it would carry no signal, but informed flow exists.
explanations.B: Makers observe their own fills and the tape, which is exactly the signal.
explanations.C: Prices move precisely because trading activity carries information.
explanations.D: Correct. Aggressive, persistent, one-sided trading often reflects a view or knowledge not yet in the quote.

questions.question: You see a burst of buying, then it stops and the price holds at the higher level. The lesson is:
options: Trade-driven information lifted fair; your updated quotes should reflect the new level.
Snap your fair back down to the old level.
The move was meaningless since it stopped.
Quotes should ignore where price settled.

explanations.A: Correct. A durable higher price after informed buying confirms fair value moved up, so keep quotes there.
explanations.B: If the price held higher, reverting fair would leave you stale-low again.
explanations.C: A move that holds is exactly the case where the information was real.
explanations.D: The settled price is your best new estimate of fair value.

questions.question: Mostly faster, better-informed traders trade against your very tight quote. Over many fills you tend to:
options: Win on average, because more volume is good.
Lose on average, because whoever trades is usually on the right side — so widen your spread.
Break even, since trades are symmetric.
Be unaffected, since spread covers everything.

explanations.A: Volume from informed counterparties is adverse; more of it means more losses.
explanations.B: Correct. When informed traders choose when to hit you, your fills are skewed against you; a wider spread compensates.
explanations.C: Informed flow is not symmetric — the counterparty trades only when it favors them.
explanations.D: A too-tight spread does not cover the cost of trading against informed flow.

questions.question: Adverse selection in market making refers to:
options: Choosing which customers you like best.
Randomly being assigned trades by the exchange.
The counterparty who trades tends to have an informational edge, skewing fills against you.
Always getting the better side of every trade.

explanations.A: Adverse selection is about information asymmetry, not customer preference.
explanations.B: It is not random assignment; it is informed counterparties self-selecting to trade.
explanations.C: Correct. You disproportionately get filled exactly when the price is about to move against you.
explanations.D: It is the opposite: you tend to get the worse side against informed flow.

questions.question: A direct way to reduce adverse-selection cost is to:
options: Tighten your spread to attract more flow.
Quote the same regardless of conditions.
Always increase your size.
Widen your spread or cut size when informed flow is likely.

explanations.A: Tightening invites even more informed flow at worse terms for you.
explanations.B: Static quoting ignores when adverse selection risk spikes.
explanations.C: More size against informed flow magnifies the loss.
explanations.D: Correct. Charging more and risking less per trade offsets the expected loss from informed counterparties.

questions.question: Quoting a very tight market right before a known reveal is risky because:
options: Informed traders will pick off the side the reveal favors before you can adjust.
Tight quotes never get traded.
The reveal cannot affect fair value.
Spreads are irrelevant near news.

explanations.A: Correct. A tight pre-news quote offers cheap optionality to anyone who anticipates the outcome.
explanations.B: Tight quotes get traded the most, which is the danger here.
explanations.C: A reveal is exactly what moves fair value, creating the pick-off.
explanations.D: Spreads matter most near news, when adverse selection is highest.

questions.question: Every time you get filled, the market immediately moves against your new position. This means:
options: You are simply unlucky; keep quoting the same.
You are being adversely selected; widen and re-check your fair value.
Your spread is too wide.
You should quote tighter to recover losses.

explanations.A: A persistent pattern is information, not luck; the quote needs to change.
explanations.B: Correct. Consistent post-fill moves against you are the signature of trading with better-informed counterparties.
explanations.C: If anything the spread is too tight to cover the adverse moves.
explanations.D: Tightening into adverse flow accelerates losses.

questions.question: How does uninformed flow differ from informed flow for a market maker?
options: Both are equally profitable.
Uninformed flow is the one that costs you.
Uninformed flow is profitable to make; informed flow imposes adverse-selection costs.
Neither type affects your P&L.

explanations.A: They are not equal — informed flow systematically costs you.
explanations.B: It is reversed: informed flow is the costly one.
explanations.C: Correct. Uninformed traders pay the spread without an edge, while informed traders extract value from you.
explanations.D: The mix of informed vs uninformed flow is central to maker P&L.

questions.question: Wider spreads help against adverse selection primarily by:
options: Guaranteeing you never trade with informed traders.
Making your quotes update automatically.
Eliminating all inventory risk.
Charging more to compensate for sometimes trading with informed counterparties.

explanations.A: Width reduces but does not eliminate informed fills; it prices them in.
explanations.B: Spread width and repricing speed are separate tools.
explanations.C: Spread does not remove inventory risk; that is a different exposure.
explanations.D: Correct. The extra spread is a premium that offsets expected losses to informed flow.

questions.question: A market maker's edge erodes most when:
options: Counterparties consistently know more than the posted quote reflects.
Counterparties are mostly uninformed.
Spreads are wide.
Quotes update too quickly.

explanations.A: Correct. Persistent information asymmetry in the counterparties' favor is what destroys maker edge.
explanations.B: Uninformed flow is profitable; it builds, not erodes, edge.
explanations.C: Wide spreads protect edge; they do not erode it.
explanations.D: Fast updates protect against pick-off; they help, not hurt.

questions.question: You suspect a single informed counterparty wants to trade large size with you. Best protection:
options: Quote tighter to win their full size.
Widen, shade away from their direction, or step back rather than keep quoting tight.
Match their direction with your own fair.
Ignore them and keep your normal quote.

explanations.A: Winning size from an informed trader at tight prices maximizes your loss.
explanations.B: Correct. Reducing exposure and pricing in their likely edge limits the damage from one informed player.
explanations.C: Blindly chasing without pricing the risk still leaves you exposed.
explanations.D: Ignoring a likely informed counterparty is how you get adversely selected in size.

questions.question: Adverse selection is worst when your quotes are:
options: Fresh and appropriately wide.
Updated faster than everyone else's.
Stale or too tight while others hold superior information.
Centered on the correct conditional mean.

explanations.A: Fresh, well-priced quotes minimize, not maximize, adverse selection.
explanations.B: Being fastest reduces adverse selection.
explanations.C: Correct. Cheap, outdated optionality plus informed counterparties is the perfect setup to be picked off.
explanations.D: Correctly centered quotes are the goal, not the danger.

questions.question: A large, clearly informed buyer is sweeping offers across the whole market. Your best response is:
options: Keep selling at the old fair to capture flow.
Lower your offers to attract the buyer.
Lower your fair value.
Raise your fair-value estimate and lift your offers before quoting again.

explanations.A: Selling into an informed buyer at stale prices is how you get run over.
explanations.B: Cutting your offer hands the informed buyer even cheaper size.
explanations.C: Aggressive buying implies higher value, not lower.
explanations.D: Correct. Aggressive informed demand signals higher value, so reprice up to avoid selling cheap.

questions.question: A big buyer lifts your 50.0 offer in size. Before requoting you should:
options: Mark fair value upward (e.g., toward 50.x and higher) because the trade itself is information.
Keep fair at 50.0 and reload the same offer.
Mark fair value down toward 49.
Leave fair unchanged but cut your offer.

explanations.A: Correct. The act of paying up in size updates your estimate of fair value, so your new quotes should be higher.
explanations.B: Reloading the same offer just sells more cheap size into informed demand.
explanations.C: A big lift points up, so marking down is backwards.
explanations.D: Cutting the offer without raising fair worsens your selling price.

questions.question: How can you tell a big informed buyer from harmless noise?
options: A single small lift that does not repeat.
Persistent size and willingness to pay up across price levels signals information.
Buying that immediately reverses.
Any trade at all, regardless of size.

explanations.A: One small, non-repeating print is closer to noise than informed size.
explanations.B: Correct. Sustained, price-insensitive buying is the hallmark of informed demand rather than random flow.
explanations.C: Flow that reverses suggests no durable information.
explanations.D: Not every trade is informed; size and persistence are what matter.

questions.question: A buyer is large but is known to be a forced, uninformed flow (e.g., a scheduled index rebalance). You should:
options: Permanently mark fair value way up.
Refuse to trade entirely.
Expect mostly temporary price impact and be willing to provide liquidity without over-updating fair.
Treat it identically to informed buying.

explanations.A: Forced, uninformed flow does not carry lasting information about fair value.
explanations.B: Uninformed forced flow is exactly the liquidity demand makers profit from supplying.
explanations.C: Correct. Mechanical, non-informational demand moves price short term but does not change long-run fair value much.
explanations.D: The whole point is that uninformed flow should be updated on less than informed flow.

questions.question: After a big informed buyer, keeping your offer unchanged means:
options: You capture extra profit per trade.
Nothing changes for your P&L.
You are protected from inventory risk.
You keep selling below the new, higher fair and bleed edge.

explanations.A: Selling below the new fair is a loss per trade, not a profit.
explanations.B: A stale-low offer into informed demand directly hurts P&L.
explanations.C: If anything you build a growing short into a rising market.
explanations.D: Correct. An unchanged offer is now stale-low relative to the updated fair, so each fill loses money.

questions.question: The size of your fair-value update after a big buyer should scale with:
options: How informed the flow looks and how much it moves consensus value.
Only the exchange fee, nothing else.
A fixed amount regardless of the flow.
The time of day only.

explanations.A: Correct. More clearly informed, larger, price-moving flow justifies a bigger update than thin or mechanical flow.
explanations.B: Fees are unrelated to how much information the flow carries.
explanations.C: A one-size update ignores how informative the particular flow is.
explanations.D: Time of day alone does not determine the information content of the flow.

questions.question: A big buyer pays through several offers quickly, then the price stabilizes higher. The takeaway:
options: You should fade it and quote back at the old level.
The trade-driven information shifted fair up, so your quotes should reflect the higher level.
The episode carried no information.
Quotes should ignore the settled price.

explanations.A: Quoting back down after the price held leaves you stale-low and exposed.
explanations.B: Correct. A price that holds at the new high confirms fair value rose, so quoting there is correct.
explanations.C: A durable price move after aggressive buying is exactly informative.
explanations.D: The settled higher price is your best new fair-value estimate.

questions.question: As you sell into a big buyer, your inventory tends to:
options: Go long automatically.
Stay flat no matter what.
Go short, adding inventory risk on top of adverse selection.
Become irrelevant to risk.

explanations.A: Selling to a buyer makes you short, not long.
explanations.B: Continuous selling moves your inventory short; it does not stay flat.
explanations.C: Correct. Filling the buyer's demand makes you progressively short while the price is rising against you.
explanations.D: A growing short into a rising market is a major risk, not irrelevant.

questions.question: After selling a lot to a big buyer you are now short while fair value has risen. A sensible response is:
options: Lower both quotes to sell even more.
Keep quotes unchanged and hope it reverts.
Widen only and never re-center.
Raise both your quotes to encourage buying stock back and reflect the higher fair.

explanations.A: Selling more deepens a losing short into a rising market.
explanations.B: Hoping for reversion while stale-short is wishful, not risk management.
explanations.C: Without re-centering up, your quotes stay mispriced for the new fair.
explanations.D: Correct. Lifting your bid and offer both reduces the short and prices in the new, higher fair value.

questions.question: After a big buyer has lifted several levels, your offer should be:
options: Higher than before — repriced up toward the new fair value.
Lower than before to win the next lift.
Exactly where it was.
Removed entirely so you never trade.

explanations.A: Correct. The informed buying raised fair value, so the offer must move up to stop selling cheap.
explanations.B: A lower offer sells even more size below the new fair.
explanations.C: Leaving the offer unchanged ignores the upward move in fair value.
explanations.D: Stepping away forever forfeits legitimate edge; the fix is to reprice, not vanish.

questions.question: One die of a two-dice sum shows 2; the other is hidden. Fair is 5.5 but the sum could be 3–8. Versus a fully-revealed sum, you should quote:
options: A tighter spread, since you now know one die.
A wider spread, because residual uncertainty from the hidden die remains.
The same spread as a fully-known sum.
No quote at all.

explanations.A: Knowing one die reduces but does not remove uncertainty; the hidden die still varies.
explanations.B: Correct. A range of 3–8 around 5.5 is real variance; a tight quote would be picked off on the tails.
explanations.C: A fully-known sum has zero uncertainty; here meaningful variance remains.
explanations.D: Residual uncertainty is normal; you still make a market, just wider.

questions.question: The more dice that remain hidden in a sum contract, the spread you quote should be:
options: Tighter, since fewer are revealed.
Unchanged, since fair value is all that matters.
Wider, since more hidden dice mean more variance around fair.
Zero, because you cannot know anything.

explanations.A: Fewer reveals means more uncertainty, which calls for a wider spread, not tighter.
explanations.B: Fair value sets the center, but spread must reflect residual variance.
explanations.C: Correct. Each hidden die adds variance, so the prudent spread grows with the number of hidden dice.
explanations.D: You still know the conditional mean; you widen, you do not stop quoting.

questions.question: Every die in the sum has now been revealed (the sum is fully known). The appropriate spread is:
options: Very wide, just to be safe.
The same as before any reveal.
Impossible to set.
Very tight, since there is no remaining uncertainty in fair value.

explanations.A: There is no residual uncertainty to protect against, so excessive width just cedes flow.
explanations.B: Full revelation removes the uncertainty that justified the original width.
explanations.C: A fully-known value is the easiest case to quote tightly.
explanations.D: Correct. With zero residual variance the only spread you need covers fees and tiny execution risk.

questions.question: Why widen your spread when residual uncertainty is large?
options: To avoid being picked off when the true value could still move far from your center.
Because wide quotes always earn more.
Because the conditional mean is unknown.
Because spreads must equal fair value.

explanations.A: Correct. Wider quotes price in the chance the realized value lands far from the conditional mean.
explanations.B: Excess width can simply lose flow; width should match uncertainty, not be maximal.
explanations.C: You do know the conditional mean; it is the residual variance you are pricing.
explanations.D: Spread width and the fair-value center are different quantities.

questions.question: Which situation carries more residual variance: two dice before any reveal, or one die shown (=4) with one hidden?
options: One die shown — knowing a die adds variance.
Two dice before any reveal — two hidden dice carry more variance than one.
They have identical variance.
Neither has any variance.

explanations.A: Revealing a die removes variance; it cannot add it.
explanations.B: Correct. More hidden randomness means more variance, so the un-revealed case is the wider one.
explanations.C: One hidden die has less variance than two hidden dice.
explanations.D: Any hidden die leaves variance; both cases have some.

questions.question: After seeing one die = 2, quoting a razor-tight market around 5.5 is dangerous because:
options: 5.5 is the wrong center.
Tight quotes can never be filled.
The true sum still ranges 3–8, so a tight quote gets picked off on the tails.
The hidden die has no effect.

explanations.A: 5.5 is the correct conditional mean; the issue is width, not center.
explanations.B: Tight quotes get filled the most, which is the danger.
explanations.C: Correct. Residual variance from the hidden die means outcomes far from 5.5 are common enough to exploit a tight quote.
explanations.D: The hidden die is exactly what creates the residual variance.

questions.question: As information accumulates and uncertainty shrinks, your spread should generally:
options: Widen, since more info is risky.
Stay fixed forever.
Jump to maximum width.
Narrow, reflecting greater confidence in fair value.

explanations.A: More information reduces uncertainty, which argues for tighter, not wider, quotes.
explanations.B: Spread should track changing uncertainty, not stay constant.
explanations.C: Falling uncertainty is the opposite of a reason to maximize width.
explanations.D: Correct. Less residual variance means less pick-off risk, so you can quote tighter.

questions.question: Spread width should primarily reflect:
options: Residual uncertainty and adverse-selection risk, not just the fair-value point.
Only the exact fair-value number.
The maximum possible payoff alone.
Nothing in particular; pick any width.

explanations.A: Correct. The center is the conditional mean; the width prices the remaining variance and informed-flow risk.
explanations.B: The fair value sets the center; width is about uncertainty and risk.
explanations.C: Max payoff is not the right gauge; dispersion around the mean is.
explanations.D: Arbitrary width ignores the uncertainty the spread is meant to price.

questions.question: Two very different outcomes (a high tail and a low tail) both remain clearly possible. You should:
options: Quote tight at the average.
Quote wide to protect against either tail.
Quote only one side.
Refuse to quote.

explanations.A: A tight quote at the mean is exposed when realized values land on either tail.
explanations.B: Correct. Large dispersion in possible outcomes means a wide spread is needed to avoid pick-off on either side.
explanations.C: One-sided quoting does not address two-sided tail risk.
explanations.D: You can still make a market; you just widen for the dispersion.

questions.question: Best practice for calibrating a quote after partial information is to:
options: Center on the unconditional mean and quote tight.
Center anywhere and keep a fixed width.
Center on the conditional mean but widen for the remaining variance.
Only adjust the center, never the width.

explanations.A: Ignoring the clue's effect on the center leaves the quote mis-centered.
explanations.B: The center must be the conditional mean and width must track uncertainty.
explanations.C: Correct. Correct center plus uncertainty-scaled width is the disciplined way to quote under partial info.
explanations.D: Residual variance means width must adapt too, not just the center.

questions.question: You privately know one die's value in a two-dice sum game; others do not. Your edge is:
options: Nonexistent — private info never helps.
Only about speed, like public info.
A reason to quote wider than everyone.
Real and one-sided — you can quote tighter or skewed toward what you know.

explanations.A: Private information is precisely what creates a genuine, durable edge.
explanations.B: Private info gives knowledge edge, not just a timing race.
explanations.C: With superior info you can often quote tighter on the side you know, not blindly wider.
explanations.D: Correct. Private information lets you price more accurately than counterparties and capture edge from their uninformed trades.

questions.question: Everyone sees the same public clue at the same instant. Your edge is now mostly:
options: Speed of repricing and spread discipline, not secret knowledge.
Secret knowledge of the true value.
Trading against your own quotes.
Quoting the widest market possible.

explanations.A: Correct. Shared information leaves no informational advantage, so execution speed and pricing discipline decide outcomes.
explanations.B: Public info is by definition not secret, so no one has hidden knowledge.
explanations.C: Self-trading is not an edge; it is meaningless.
explanations.D: Maximal width just cedes flow; the edge is acting first.

questions.question: Acting on a public clue more slowly than your rivals leads to:
options: A consistent pricing advantage.
Being picked off on stale quotes.
Lower adverse selection.
Tighter effective spreads.

explanations.A: Lagging gives a disadvantage, not an advantage.
explanations.B: Correct. While you lag, your quote reflects old fair value and is exposed to faster traders.
explanations.C: Slowness increases, not decreases, adverse fills.
explanations.D: Lagging does not tighten spreads; it leaves them mispriced.

questions.question: Genuine private information lets a market maker:
options: Guarantee a profit on every single trade.
Ignore fair value entirely.
Skew quotes toward their informational view and profit from less-informed flow.
Quote randomly without risk.

explanations.A: Even with an edge, individual trades vary; the edge is statistical.
explanations.B: Private info refines fair value; it does not let you ignore it.
explanations.C: Correct. Knowing more than the market lets you price the favored side aggressively and earn from uninformed counterparties.
explanations.D: Random quoting throws away the very edge the information provides.

questions.question: If your supposed 'edge' is information that everyone already has, then:
options: It is your strongest possible edge.
You should bet your largest size on it.
It guarantees you beat faster traders.
It is not really an edge, because the price already reflects it.

explanations.A: Shared information cannot be an edge; only differential knowledge or speed is.
explanations.B: Sizing up on non-edge is a way to lose, not win.
explanations.C: If anything, with common info the faster traders beat you.
explanations.D: Correct. Widely-known information is impounded in the quote, leaving no informational advantage.

questions.question: Raw speed matters most as an edge when:
options: Information is public and everyone races to reprice.
You have unique private information.
Nothing is happening in the market.
You have decided never to trade.

explanations.A: Correct. With shared information the only differentiator is who updates and quotes first.
explanations.B: With private info, your knowledge edge matters more than pure speed.
explanations.C: Speed is irrelevant when there is no new information to react to.
explanations.D: If you never trade, speed is moot.

questions.question: The danger of trading on information that others also possess is that:
options: Common information is always profitable.
You may be the slow one and get adversely selected.
It removes all competition.
It guarantees you are the fastest.

explanations.A: Common information offers no informational edge and can be a trap if you are slow.
explanations.B: Correct. Common information becomes a speed race, and the slower participant ends up holding stale quotes.
explanations.C: Shared information typically intensifies competition, not removes it.
explanations.D: Possessing the info says nothing about your relative speed.

questions.question: A genuine informational edge tends to show up over time as:
options: Winning literally every trade.
Random, edgeless results.
Consistently being on the right side versus uninformed counterparties.
Losing to uninformed traders.

explanations.A: Even a strong edge loses some individual trades; it is statistical.
explanations.B: Random results indicate no edge at all.
explanations.C: Correct. A real edge manifests as a positive average outcome against less-informed flow, not as any single trade.
explanations.D: Losing to uninformed flow is the opposite of having an edge.

questions.question: After a clue, you assess the probability of the high state at 0.75. The asset is worth 120 in the high state and 80 in the low state. Fair value?

explanations.A: 120 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the low-state chance.
explanations.B: 80 assumes the low state is certain, ignoring the high-state chance.
explanations.C: 100 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated probability of 0.75.

questions.question: After a clue, you assess the probability of the high state at 0.75. The asset is worth 110 in the high state and 90 in the low state. Fair value?

explanations.B: 110 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the low-state chance.
explanations.C: 90 assumes the low state is certain, ignoring the high-state chance.
explanations.D: 100 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated probability of 0.75.

questions.question: After a clue, you assess the probability of the high state at 0.25. The asset is worth 120 in the high state and 80 in the low state. Fair value?

explanations.A: 120 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the low-state chance.
explanations.C: 80 assumes the low state is certain, ignoring the high-state chance.
explanations.D: 100 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated probability of 0.25.

questions.question: After a clue, you assess the probability of the high state at 0.8. The asset is worth 100 in the high state and 0 in the low state. Fair value?

explanations.A: 100 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the low-state chance.
explanations.B: 0 assumes the low state is certain, ignoring the high-state chance.
explanations.D: 50 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated probability of 0.8.

questions.question: After a clue, you assess the probability of the high state at 0.6. The asset is worth 10 in the high state and 0 in the low state. Fair value?

explanations.A: 10 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the low-state chance.
explanations.B: 0 assumes the low state is certain, ignoring the high-state chance.
explanations.C: 5 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated probability of 0.6.

questions.question: After a clue, you assess the probability of the high state at 1/3. The asset is worth 90 in the high state and 60 in the low state. Fair value?

explanations.B: 90 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the low-state chance.
explanations.C: 60 assumes the low state is certain, ignoring the high-state chance.
explanations.D: 75 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated probability of 1/3.

questions.question: After a clue, you assess the probability of the high state at 2/3. The asset is worth 90 in the high state and 60 in the low state. Fair value?

explanations.A: 90 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the low-state chance.
explanations.C: 60 assumes the low state is certain, ignoring the high-state chance.
explanations.D: 75 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated probability of 2/3.

questions.question: After a clue, you assess the probability of the high state at 0.2. The asset is worth 200 in the high state and 100 in the low state. Fair value?

explanations.A: 200 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the low-state chance.
explanations.B: 100 assumes the low state is certain, ignoring the high-state chance.
explanations.D: 150 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated probability of 0.2.

questions.question: After a clue, you assess the probability of the high state at 0.9. The asset is worth 50 in the high state and 30 in the low state. Fair value?

explanations.A: 50 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the low-state chance.
explanations.B: 30 assumes the low state is certain, ignoring the high-state chance.
explanations.C: 40 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated probability of 0.9.

questions.question: A signal occurs 80% of the time in the high state and 40% of the time in the low state. The prior is 50/50. After seeing the signal, what is P(high)?

explanations.B: 1/2 is the prior; it ignores that the signal is twice as likely in the high state.
explanations.C: 0.8 is the likelihood of the signal given high, not the posterior probability of high.
explanations.D: 0.4 is the likelihood under the low state, not the posterior.

questions.question: A coin is fair (P(H)=1/2) or double-headed (P(H)=1), prior 50/50. It is flipped twice and shows heads both times. P(double-headed)?

explanations.A: 2/3 is the posterior after only one head, not two.
explanations.C: 1/2 is the prior, ignoring the two-heads evidence.
explanations.D: Two heads is still possible from the fair coin, so it is not certain.

questions.question: A big buyer arrives and you revise the probability of the high state from 0.5 up to 0.7. High = 120, low = 80. New fair value?

explanations.A: 100 is the prior fair value at 0.5; it ignores the upward revision to 0.7.
explanations.B: 120 assumes the high state is now certain.
explanations.D: 112 over-weights the high state beyond the stated 0.7.

questions.question: Order flow shifts your probability of the high state to 0.9. High = 50, low = 30. New fair value?

explanations.A: 50 assumes the high state is certain, ignoring the 10% low chance.
explanations.B: 40 is the 50/50 average, ignoring the updated 0.9 probability.
explanations.C: 32 applies 0.9 to the low state by mistake.

questions.question: Conditional expectation given a clue is another name for:
options: The Bayesian (posterior) fair value.
The unconditional mean.
The maximum possible payoff.
A random guess.

explanations.A: Correct. Updating fair value to the expectation under the posterior distribution is exactly Bayesian fair value.
explanations.B: The unconditional mean ignores the clue; the conditional expectation uses it.
explanations.C: Conditional expectation is an average, not the maximum.
explanations.D: It is a precise computation from the updated distribution, not a guess.

questions.question: A signal is equally likely under the high and low states (uninformative). After observing it, the posterior:
options: Jumps to certainty of the high state.
Equals the prior — fair value does not move.
Always halves the prior.
Becomes zero.

explanations.A: An uninformative signal provides no reason to favor either state.
explanations.B: Correct. If the signal carries no discriminating information, Bayes' rule leaves the probabilities unchanged.
explanations.C: An uninformative signal leaves the prior unchanged, not halved.
explanations.D: An uninformative signal does not drive any probability to zero.

questions.question: Stronger evidence in favor of the high state should move your fair value:
options: Closer to the low-state value.
Exactly to the midpoint.
Closer to the high-state value.
Nowhere — evidence is irrelevant.

explanations.A: Evidence for high pulls fair toward high, not low.
explanations.B: The midpoint corresponds to 50/50, not to stronger high evidence.
explanations.C: Correct. More evidence raises the posterior probability of high, pulling the expectation toward the high value.
explanations.D: Evidence is exactly what updates fair value in Bayesian pricing.

questions.question: Prior P(high) = 0.25. A signal is three times as likely under the high state as the low state. After the signal, P(high)?

explanations.A: 0.75 confuses the likelihood ratio with the posterior probability.
explanations.B: 0.25 is the prior; it ignores the favorable likelihood ratio.
explanations.C: Multiplying the prior by a fraction is not how Bayes' rule normalizes.

questions.question: Two equally likely states value an asset at 90 (high) and 60 (low). A clue raises P(high) to 2/3. Fair value?

explanations.B: 75 is the 50/50 prior fair, ignoring the update to 2/3.
explanations.C: 90 assumes the high state is certain.
explanations.D: 70 applies 2/3 to the low state by mistake.

questions.question: Why does a big informed buyer justify a Bayesian fair-value increase?
options: Because trades never carry information.
The buying is evidence that raises the posterior probability of the high state.
Because fair value must always rise over time.
Because the prior is always wrong.

explanations.A: If trades carried no information there would be no reason to update at all.
explanations.B: Correct. Treating the trade as a signal updates your probabilities upward, which lifts the expected value.
explanations.C: Fair value moves with evidence in either direction, not mechanically upward.
explanations.D: Bayesian updating refines the prior with evidence; it does not assume the prior is wrong.
